# misfits science index

The forward map: **science → misfit**. Inverted from the Origin table every misfit's `REFERENCE.md` carries, so navigation and the per-misfit warrant share one source and cannot drift. 113 misfits, 570 scholars.

What the mechanism does not claim. |

The Mobility Gap, the Evaporation. |

The play's spine and its name. |

The Feed, the Attending. |

| ↳ | `not_invented_here` | Managing the Flow of Technology (1977) | The gatekeeper: the rare person who reads outside sources and translates them inward across the boundary. The Gatekeeper, the Bridge. |

The Detachment. |

The governing frame. The Objective, The Showing. |

The play's governing law. |

The Man-Month, the Slip. |
| ↳ | `the_ninth_woman` | MMM on intercommunication overhead scaling as n(n-1)/2 <br><sub>Brooks (communication)</sub> | The cross-talk: each added person adds channels faster than capacity, so coordination grows as the square of the team while output does not. The Cross-Talk. |

| ↳ | `the_anticommons` | "Symmetric Tragedies: Commons and Anticommons", Journal of Law and Economics (2000) <br><sub>Buchanan & Yoon</sub> | The symmetry proved: the commons underprices access and is overused; the anticommons overprices it and is underused. | ↳ | `vetocracy` | The Calculus of Consent (1962) <br><sub>Buchanan & Tullock</sub> | The consent-cost tradeoff: as the assent required rises toward unanimity, decision costs explode toward paralysis. The Asymmetry. |

The governing frame. The Objective, The Showing. |

| **Henrich** | `the_streetlight_effect` | "The Weirdest People in the World?" (Behavioral and Brain Sciences, 2010) <br><sub>Henrich et al.</sub> | The reachable sample: a science built on the accessible, Western and educated, subjects, generalised as if universal. The Light, the Dark. |
| **Herbsleb** | `the_mirror` | "Splitting the Organization and Integrating the Code: Conway's Law Revisited", ICSE (1999) <br><sub>Herbsleb & Grinter</sub> | The cost: coordination across an organisational boundary is thin and expensive, so integration is hardest exactly where two teams meet. The Boundary, the Handoff. |
| **Hernán** | `the_false_trade_off` | "A Structural Approach to Selection Bias" (Epidemiology, 2004) <br><sub>Hernán, Hernández-Díaz & Robins</sub> | Selection as collider: selection bias recast as collider-stratification bias, the act of selecting or conditioning on a common effect of exposure and outcome biasing the association between them. The Sample. |
| **Hernández-Díaz** | `the_false_trade_off` | "A Structural Approach to Selection Bias" (Epidemiology, 2004) <br><sub>Hernán, Hernández-Díaz & Robins</sub> | Selection as collider: selection bias recast as collider-stratification bias, the act of selecting or conditioning on a common effect of exposure and outcome biasing the association between them. The Sample. |
| **Hirsch** | `the_arms_race` | Social Limits to Growth (1976): positional goods | The naming: goods scarce in relative terms, whose supply cannot grow to meet demand, so competition for them is inescapable. The play's governing law, the Rank. |
| **Hirschman** | `the_loyalty_trap` | Exit, Voice, and Loyalty: Responses to Decline in Firms, Organizations, and States (1970) | The spine: exit and voice as the two responses to decline, and loyalty as the factor that conditions them, holding both when it grows strong. The governing law. |
| ↳ | `the_loyalty_trap` | on loyalty's double role: it delays exit to give voice time, yet can suppress voice and postpone all response | The double edge: loyalty is functional when it converts exit into voice, pathological when it converts both into silence. The Faith. |

The play's governing law. |
| ↳ | `information_cascade` | "Learning from the Behavior of Others: Conformity, Fads, and Informational Cascades", Journal of Economic Perspectives (1998) <br><sub>Bikhchandani, Hirshleifer & Welch</sub> | The fragility: cascades rest on little information and shift abruptly when new public information arrives. The Fragility, the Turn. |
| **Hochschild** | `the_front` | The Managed Heart (University of California Press, 1983) | The cost: emotional labour, the managed presentation of feeling as the price of the role; the sustained private cost of holding the front. The Managed Heart. |
| **Hollnagel** | `the_brittle_optimum` | The ETTO Principle: Efficiency-Thoroughness Trade-Off (2009) | The trade: systems and people trade thoroughness for efficiency, eroding the very margins that keep them safe. The Margin, the Trim. |
| **Holmström** | `metric_fixation` | "Multitask Principal-Agent Analyses: Incentive Contracts, Asset Ownership, and Job Design", JLEO (1991) <br><sub>Holmström & Milgrom</sub> | The multitasking distortion: rewarding a measured dimension pulls effort off the unmeasured ones, which is where the rest of the goal lived. The Unmeasured, formalised. |
| ↳ | `moral_hazard` | "Moral Hazard and Observability", Bell Journal of Economics (1979); Nobel Prize in Economics (2016) | Hidden action: when the principal cannot observe the agent's effort, the optimal contract trades insurance against incentive, and no contract restores first-best care. The Hidden Hand. |
| **Holt** | `information_cascade` | "Information Cascades in the Laboratory", American Economic Review (1997) <br><sub>Anderson & Holt</sub> | The experiment: subjects form cascades as the theory predicts, rationally following actions against their own signals, and are often wrong. The Public Action. |
| **Holtom** | `the_dead_sea_effect` | "Why People Stay: Using Job Embeddedness to Predict Voluntary Turnover", Academy of Management Journal (2001) <br><sub>Mitchell, Holtom & Lee</sub> | Who stays and why: embeddedness, links, fit, and cost of leaving, holds people in place, including good ones, so not every stayer is residue. | **Hopp** | `no_idle_hands` | Factory Physics (1996) <br><sub>Hopp & Spearman</sub> | Operationalised: the VUT relation, variability times a utilisation factor times the process time, and utilisation buffered against variability by slack. The Slack, The Jam. |
| **Hopper** | `the_hollow_word` | Grammaticalization (Cambridge University Press, 1993; 2nd edn 2003) <br><sub>Hopper & Traugott</sub> | Semantic bleaching: the loss of semantic content through repeated use, sense generalising and thinning as a form is used. The Bleach, The Word. |
| **Hora** | `the_bus_factor` | "A Novel Approach for Estimating Truck Factors", ICPC (2016); Cosentino et al., "Assessing the Bus Factor of Git Repositories", SANER (2015) <br><sub>Avelino, Hora & Valente</sub> | Empirical measurement of the truck / bus factor: most projects concentrate their knowledge in one or two contributors. The Concentration, measured. |
| **Hsee** | `the_tyranny_of_the_urgent` | "The Mere Urgency Effect" (Journal of Consumer Research, 2018) <br><sub>Zhu, Yang, and Hsee</sub> | The spine: people choose urgent-but-less-rewarding tasks over important ones merely for the sense of expiration, urgency capturing effort against payoff. The governing law. |
| **Hull** | `rising_to_unfit` | "The Peter Principle: Why Things Always Go Wrong" (1969) <br><sub>Peter & Hull</sub> | The naming and the law: in a hierarchy, employees rise to their level of incompetence, so in time every post tends to be held by someone unfit for it. The play's spine. |
| ↳ | `the_dilbert_principle` | The Peter Principle (1969): promotion to the level of incompetence <br><sub>Peter and Hull</sub> | The foil: the inverse mechanism the house stages as Rising to Unfit, cited to mark the Dilbert Principle apart, failure promoted rather than success. |
| **Hummel** | `the_tyranny_of_the_urgent` | Tyranny of the Urgent (1967) <br><sub>Charles E. Hummel</sub> | The name and phenomenology: the urgent crowding out the important, the task that calls for instant action against the one that merely matters. The Deadline, the Undone. |
| **Hutto** | `the_lie_of_the_land` | "A Framework for Understanding Ecological Traps and an Evaluation of Existing Evidence" (Ecology, 2006) <br><sub>Robertson & Hutto</sub> | The mechanisms: equal-preference and severe traps, the attractive cue persisting after quality falls, the poor site preferred even above the good. The Quality, The Preference, The Apparent Fit. |
| **Huy** | `the_frozen_middle` | "In Praise of Middle Managers", HBR (2001); "Emotional Balancing of Organizational Continuity and Radical Change", ASQ (2002) | The middle balances continuity and change: its continuity work keeps the operation running through disruption, and that same buffering is what dampens the change. The play's no-villain spine. |
| **Ibbotson** | `the_survivors` | "Survivorship Bias in Performance Studies", Review of Financial Studies (1992) <br><sub>Brown, Goetzmann, Ibbotson & Ross</sub> | Survivorship in finance: dead and delisted funds drop out, so studies of the survivors overstate returns and manufacture false persistence. The Denominator and the Lesson. |
| **Iliev** | `moral_licensing` | "Sinning Saints and Saintly Sinners: The Paradox of Moral Self-Regulation" (Psychological Science, 2009) <br><sub>Sachdeva, Iliev & Medin</sub> | The set point: morality regulated toward a level, so good licenses and bad cleanses. The Balance. |
| **Illich** | `first_do_harm` | Medical Nemesis (1976); the expropriation of health; the mystification of iatrogenic harm | The alibi and the nemesis: harm absorbed as new sickness rather than traced to its source, and the system disabling the capacity to cope. The Alibi, The Nemesis. |
| ↳ | `first_do_harm` | Medical Nemesis / Limits to Medicine (1976); clinical, social, and cultural iatrogenesis | The spine: the injury done by the healing itself, from the wound of a single intervention to the enterprise whose harm exceeds its cure. The Remedy, The Wound, The Nemesis. |

| **Ioannidis** | `the_file_drawer` | "Why Most Published Research Findings Are False" (PLoS Medicine, 2005) <br><sub>John Ioannidis</sub> | The aggregate consequence: on a probabilistic argument, selection for the significant, joined to analytic flexibility and low power, means the published literature systematically overstates, so that most claimed findings, he argues, will not hold, the distortion living in the pooled record and not in any one study. The Inflation. |
| **Isenberg** | `the_filter_bubble` | group-polarization meta-analysis (1986); deliberating-enclaves work <br><sub>Isenberg; Sunstein</sub> | The drift: a like-minded group, deliberating only among itself, moving to a more extreme version of its prior view. The Polarisation. |
| **Island** | `alarm_fatigue` | EEMUA 191 and ISA-18.2 alarm-management standards; the 1979 control-room alarm flood <br><sub>EEMUA; ISA / Three Mile Island</sub> | The industrial frame: alarm floods overwhelming operators in process control, and the management practice built to answer them. | **Jackson** | `the_double_bind` | "Toward a Theory of Schizophrenia" (Behavioral Science, 1956) <br><sub>Bateson, Jackson, Haley, Weakland</sub> | The spine: the double bind itself, the contradictory injunction in an inescapable field with metacommunication forbidden, and its ingredients. | ↳ | `the_double_bind` | Pragmatics of Human Communication (1967); levels of message, metacommunication, the paradoxical injunction <br><sub>Watzlawick, Beavin, and Jackson</sub> | The extension: content and relationship levels of a message, and the "be spontaneous" paradox that cannot be met on demand. | ↳ | `the_identified_patient` | "The Question of Family Homeostasis" (Psychiatric Quarterly Supplement, 1957) <br><sub>Don D. Jackson</sub> | The homeostasis: the system's resistance to change, the recovery that destabilises the whole and the new bearer recruited. The Balance, and the pull back. |

Scott's acknowledged forerunner. The City. |
| **James** | `the_best_on_paper` | "Inadmissibility of the Usual Estimator..." (Stein, 1956); "Estimation with Quadratic Loss" (James and Stein, 1961) <br><sub>Stein; James & Stein</sub> | The shrinkage: pulling three or more estimates toward a common mean dominates taking them at face value, the statistical form of the remedy that shrinks the boldest reading back toward the prior. | ↳ | `the_trip_to_abilene` | "Victims of Groupthink" (1972); "Groupthink" (1982) | Groupthink: self-censorship and the illusion of unanimity, where silence is read as consent and doubts go unvoiced to preserve cohesion. The Swallowed Objection and the Mandate. |

| ↳ | `jevons_paradox` | "The Coal Question" (1865) | The paradox and the name: it is a confusion to suppose the economical use of fuel means diminished consumption; the contrary is the truth, as efficiency drove coal use up. The spine. |

| ↳ | `the_planning_fallacy` | Thinking, Fast and Slow (2011): the kitchen renovation, the textbook that took years | The persistence: the fallacy holds even for those who know it and have overrun before, because the inside view is the natural way to see one's own task. |

| ↳ | `the_streetlight_effect` | The Conduct of Inquiry (1964), "the principle of the drunkard's search" | The spine and the parable: the keys sought under the lamp because the light is better there. The play's governing law. |

| ↳ | `path_dependence` | "Network Externalities, Competition, and Compatibility", American Economic Review (1985) <br><sub>Katz & Shapiro</sub> | The network effect: the value of a choice rises with the number who share it, so adopters coordinate on the leader. The Network, the Early Lead. |

| **Kemeny** | `accidental_adversaries` | the "Accidental Adversaries" systems archetype (credited originator) | The spine and the name: partners whose defensive moves undermine each other into rivalry. The play's governing law. |

| ↳ | `social_loafing` | "Dispensability of member effort and group motivation losses: Free-rider effects", JPSP (1983); Kerr, "Motivation losses in small groups: A social dilemma analysis", JPSP (1983) <br><sub>Kerr & Bruun; Kerr</sub> | The free-rider and sucker effects: members reduce effort when it seems dispensable (Kerr & Bruun), and the hard worker reduces effort to avoid being the sucker who carries them (Kerr). The play's governing law. |
| ↳ | `the_paradox_of_thrift` | Kahn, "The Relation of Home Investment to Unemployment" (Economic Journal, 1931) <br><sub>Kahn; Keynes</sub> | The multiplier: a withdrawal of spending contracts income by a multiple of itself, so the leak compounds. The Contraction. |
| ↳ | `the_paradox_of_thrift` | The General Theory of Employment, Interest and Money (1936) | The spine: saving as a withdrawal from the circular flow, aggregate demand, and the paradox that a greater desire to save can reduce income and saving alike. The play's governing law, the Circular Flow. |

| **Khurana** | `the_leadership_script` | Searching for a Corporate Savior (2002); the market for the charismatic leader | The selection: the elite market choosing leaders on charismatic image over the demonstrable record, the legible sound over the illegible work. The Ladder. |

The play's governing law. |

(1970); the diffusion of responsibility <br><sub>Latane and Darley</sub> | Social shirking: responsibility diffusing across a group so each is less likely to act, the mechanism Sagan invokes for redundant guards. The Reliance. |

| ↳ | `the_success_trap` | "Organizational Learning", Annual Review of Sociology (1988); "The Myopia of Learning", Strategic Management Journal (1993) <br><sub>Levitt & March; Levinthal & March</sub> | The naming: the competency trap, favourable experience with an inferior routine crowding out the better one (Levitt & March), and the myopia by which learning over-weights the near and past success (Levinthal & March). | **Noelle-Neumann** | `the_trip_to_abilene` | "The Spiral of Silence: A Theory of Public Opinion" (1974); book (1984) | The spiral of silence: fear of isolation makes those who sense they are in the minority go quiet, which emboldens the apparent majority, which quiets them further. The Silence Travels. |
| **Nonaka** | `the_bus_factor` | "The Knowledge-Creating Company" (1995) <br><sub>Nonaka & Takeuchi</sub> | Tacit versus explicit knowledge and the cost of converting one to the other: why capturing what an expert knows is slow, effortful, and easily deferred. |
| ↳ | `the_frozen_middle` | "Toward Middle-Up-Down Management", Sloan Management Review (1988) | The middle as the knot where top vision meets frontline reality: when the knot buffers instead of joining, the two ends never reach each other. The Frozen Middle. |
| **Nordhaus** | `baumols_cost_disease` | "Baumol's Diseases: A Macroeconomic Perspective", B.E. Journal of Macroeconomics (2008) | The evidence: across US industries, stagnant-productivity sectors show the predicted rising relative prices and growing spending shares. The Rising Cost. |
| **Norman** | `the_decision_travels` | "The Design of Everyday Things" (1988) | User-centred design and the gulf of execution and evaluation: what happens when a design assumes an idealised user instead of the one in the room. |

(kidney stones, 1986) <br><sub>Bickel, Hammel & O'Connell</sub> | The cases in the world: admissions and treatment data where the aggregate reverses every subgroup, and a decision hangs on the level. The Overbid. |
| **Odum** | `the_tyranny_of_small_decisions` | "Environmental Degradation and the Tyranny of Small Decisions" (BioScience, 1982) | The ecological form: wetlands and open land lost parcel by parcel, no one deciding to destroy the whole. The Irreversibility, the Vacancy. |
| **Ohno** | `the_brittle_optimum` | Taiichi Ohno, the Toyota Production System; the drive to eliminate waste (muda), buffers, inventory <br><sub>Ohno; lean</sub> | The driver: the efficiency case for cutting every idle buffer, the reward that makes each trim rational. The Trimming, the Efficiency. |

The governing frame. The Objective, The Showing. |
| **Olson** | `bootleggers_and_baptists` | The Logic of Collective Action (Harvard University Press, 1965) <br><sub>Mancur Olson</sub> | The asymmetry: the concentrated interest organises to defend a rule where the diffuse public, feeling the cost too thinly, will not, so the defence is resourced on the side of the gain and unorganised on the side of the cost, and the coalition forms lopsided. | ↳ | `regulatory_capture` | "The Logic of Collective Action" (1965) | Concentrated versus diffuse interests: small groups with large per-member stakes organise; large groups with small per-member stakes do not. The Concentrated Interest, the Public. |
| ↳ | `the_commons` | The Logic of Collective Action (1965) | Free riding: public goods are under-provided because their benefit cannot be withheld, so each does best to let others bear the upkeep. The Free Ride, the play's second engine. |
| ↳ | `vetocracy` | The Rise and Decline of Nations (1982) | Institutional sclerosis: the accumulation of veto-holding distributional coalitions that gums up collective action. The Thicket. |
| **Osgood** | `accidental_adversaries` | An Alternative to War or Surrender (1962), GRIT | The unilateral step: graduated, reciprocated de-escalation to break a hostile spiral by stopping first. The Mender, See the Whole. |
| **Ostrom** | `the_commons` | Governing the Commons (1990); Nobel Prize in Economics (2009) | The answer to inevitability: enduring commons are governed by design principles, clear bounds, home-made rules, monitoring, graduated sanctions. The Bylaw, the Warden, the Enclosure. |

The Reliability, the Drift. |
| ↳ | `the_empty_seat` | "Humans and Automation: Use, Misuse, Disuse, Abuse" (Human Factors, 1997) <br><sub>Parasuraman & Riley</sub> | The spine: misuse as overtrust, the operator relying on automation beyond what its reliability warrants. The governing frame. |
| ↳ | `the_irony_of_automation` | "Humans and Automation: Use, Misuse, Disuse, Abuse" (Human Factors, 1997) <br><sub>Parasuraman and Riley</sub> | The complacency: over-reliance and automation bias, the monitor's attention eroding on a system that rarely fails. The Drift. |
| **Parducci** | `prevalence_induced_concept_change` | range-frequency theory of category judgment (1965) | The calibration: category judgments depend on the range and frequency of the context, so a shifted context shifts the boundary. The Margin. |
| **Pariser** | `the_filter_bubble` | The Filter Bubble: What the Internet Is Hiding from You (2011) | The frame: the personalised feed that gives each of us our own edited world, the opposing view filtered out unremarked because it was never clicked. The Filter. |
| **Parkinson** | `pournelles_iron_law` | Parkinson's Law (1955) | Headcount as the coin: officials multiply subordinates and make work for one another, growth as internal advancement. The Internal Currency, the Turn Inward. |
| ↳ | `the_colour_of_the_bike_shed` | "Parkinson's Law of Triviality", in Parkinson's Law, or the Pursuit of Progress (1957) | The parable and the law: a committee approves a nuclear reactor in minutes and debates a bicycle shed for an hour, spending time in inverse proportion to cost. The play's spine and name. |
| ↳ | `the_colour_of_the_bike_shed` | "Parkinson's Law": work expands so as to fill the time available for its completion (1955, 1957) | The expanding item: a discussion open to all grows to consume the whole slot, ended by the clock and not the question. |
| ↳ | `the_planning_fallacy` | "Parkinson's Law" (The Economist, 1955) | The far arm: work expands to fill the time available, so a buffer added too freely is consumed rather than saved. The Padding. |
| ↳ | `the_shirky_principle` | Parkinson's Law, or the Pursuit of Progress (1957), the law of bureaucratic growth | Self-growth: officials multiply and work expands to justify the establishment, independent of whether the underlying problem shrinks. The Expander. |

The Ratchet, The Selecting. |
| **Pauly** | `moral_hazard` | "The Economics of Moral Hazard: Comment", American Economic Review (1968) | The no-villain spine: the response is not a moral defect but a rational reaction to a zero marginal price, so it persists among honest, sensible people. The Free Downside. |
| **Pearl** | `the_bigger_picture` | Causality (2000, 2009); the causal resolution and the confounder-versus-mediator distinction <br><sub>Judea Pearl</sub> | The resolution and its cost: the level to trust is a causal choice the data cannot make, and the wrong model reverses the reversal. | **Peckham** | `social_loafing` | "The Ringelmann effect: Studies of group size and group performance", JESP (1974) <br><sub>Ingham, Levinger, Graves & Peckham</sub> | Coordination versus motivation: separates lost effort from mere mis-timing, isolating the motivational drop that survives when coordination is controlled. | **Peltzman** | `regulatory_capture` | "Toward a More General Theory of Regulation", Journal of Law and Economics (1976) | The balance of pressure: the regulator weighs organised interest groups, and the unorganised lose, so outcomes track who can bring pressure. The Lobby. |
| ↳ | `the_spent_margin` | "The Effects of Automobile Safety Regulation" (Journal of Political Economy, 1975) <br><sub>Sam Peltzman</sub> | The spine and the name: mandated safety devices offset by more intensive driving, so occupant deaths fall while the total toll barely moves and the risk shifts onto pedestrians and motorcyclists. Peltzman's own claim was a near-complete offset; the play's governing law is the mechanism, and the disputed magnitude is tempered below. |

| ↳ | `the_brittle_optimum` | Normal Accidents: Living with High-Risk Technologies (1984) | The coupling: tight coupling and interactive complexity make accidents normal, and removing slack and buffers tightens the coupling. The Coupling, the Shatter. |

| ↳ | `the_dilbert_principle` | The Peter Principle (1969): promotion to the level of incompetence <br><sub>Peter and Hull</sub> | The foil: the inverse mechanism the house stages as Rising to Unfit, cited to mark the Dilbert Principle apart, failure promoted rather than success. |

| **Phelps** | `the_proxy` | "The Statistical Theory of Racism and Sexism" (American Economic Review, 1972) <br><sub>Edmund Phelps</sub> | The spine and the name: under uncertainty about individual quality, an agent rationally judges the individual by the observable group average, so unlike people are priced by the group they belong to. The play's governing law. |
| **Pigou** | `the_extra_road` | The Economics of Welfare (1920) | The mechanism: congestion is an unpriced externality, the cost each user imposes on the rest; a toll on the shared road aligns private and social cost. The Congestion, the Toll. |

| **Polanyi** | `chestertons_fence` | The Tacit Dimension (1966) | The unwritten reason: we know more than we can tell, so the knowledge a safeguard encodes may be real and unarticulable, present in the practice and absent from any record. The Forgotten Reason. |
| ↳ | `the_bus_factor` | "The Tacit Dimension" (1966) | Tacit knowledge: "we know more than we can tell", so a large part of expertise resists being written down and stays in the person who holds it. The Tacit Knowledge. |

The Drawer. |

| **Stephens** | `safety_in_numbers` | "What is the Allee effect?" (Oikos, 1999) <br><sub>Stephens, Sutherland & Freckleton</sub> | The definition: the Allee effect as a positive relationship between per-capita growth rate and density, and the critical density that marks it. The Critical Number. |

The play's governing law. |
| **Sterman** | `fixes_that_fail` | Business Dynamics: Systems Thinking and Modeling for a Complex World (2000) | The worse-before-better tradeoff: side effects, policy resistance, and why the corrective move looks like failure while it works. The Reformer, the cost of the cure. |
| ↳ | `shifting_the_burden` | "Business Dynamics: Systems Thinking and Modeling for a Complex World" (2000) | The formal account: the balancing loops of the two solutions and the reinforcing loop of dependence, modelled, and why delay and erosion lock the system onto the symptomatic path. |
| ↳ | `shifting_the_burden` | "Nobody Ever Gets Credit for Fixing Problems that Never Happened", California Management Review (2001) <br><sub>Repenning & Sterman</sub> | The capability trap: firefighting is visible and rewarded, capability-building is invisible and starved, so organisations tilt toward the quick fix and erode the capacity to prevent. Why the fix keeps winning. |
| ↳ | `the_bullwhip_effect` | "Modeling Managerial Behavior" and the Beer Distribution Game (Management Science, 1989) | The experiment: players generate the bullwhip even with steady demand, from misperception of feedback and delays. The Lag. |
| ↳ | `the_firefighters_medal` | "Capability Traps and Self-Confirming Attribution Errors in the Dynamics of Process Improvement" (ASQ, 2002) <br><sub>Repenning and Sterman</sub> | The tip: the reinforcing loop and the tipping point, and the attribution error by which managers misread the trap as a people problem. The Spiral. |
| ↳ | `the_firefighters_medal` | "Nobody Ever Gets Credit for Fixing Problems that Never Happened" (California Management Review, 2001) <br><sub>Repenning and Sterman</sub> | The spine: the reward asymmetry and the bistable capability dynamic, effort draining from prevention into firefighting until the system tips. The governing law. |

The other half of the remedy, and its limits. |
| **Stone** | `overfitting` | "Cross-Validatory Choice and Assessment of Statistical Predictions" (JRSS B, 1974) | The held-back test: data set aside to read the gap before deployment. The Holdout. |
| **Strathern** | `metric_fixation` | Goodhart's Law (1975); Marilyn Strathern's formulation, "Improving ratings: audit in the British University system" (1997) <br><sub>Goodhart / Strathern</sub> | When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure: the act of targeting the proxy is what breaks its link to the goal. The play's central law. |
| **Strotz** | `time_inconsistency` | "Myopia and Inconsistency in Dynamic Utility Maximization", Review of Economic Studies (1955-56) | The origin: a plan optimal now is abandoned later by the same planner, and only precommitment holds it. The Temptation, the intrapersonal Ulysses. |
| **Studdert** | `the_paper_trail` | "Defensive Medicine Among High-Risk Specialist Physicians", JAMA (2005) <br><sub>Studdert et al.</sub> | The canonical case: physicians ordering tests and procedures to reduce liability rather than to help patients, work aimed at the chart and not the care. The Cover, the Displacement. |
| **Sull** | `the_frozen_middle` | "Why Good Companies Go Bad", HBR (1999); active inertia | Active inertia: an organisation responds to change by accelerating its established routines, so effort looks like motion and delivers continuity. Why the layer freezes. |
| **Summers** | `nothing_to_lose` | "The Case of the Disappearing Defendant: An Economic Analysis" (1983) | Full liability meeting an empty pocket: the defendant whose worth is less than the harm, the judgment awarded and uncollectible. The Judgment, The Excess. |
| **Sunstein** | `the_availability_cascade` | "Availability Cascades and Risk Regulation" (Stanford Law Review, 1999) <br><sub>Kuran and Sunstein</sub> | The spine: the availability cascade itself, the informational and reputational chain that inflates a risk perception and drives regulation by salience. The governing law. |
| ↳ | `the_availability_cascade` | Laws of Fear: Beyond the Precautionary Principle (2005); cost-benefit and insulated risk assessment | The response and its check: regulation set by public fear, and the weighing of magnitude that would correct it, at a cost to those who insist on it. The Overreach, the Cold Number. |
| ↳ | `the_filter_bubble` | group-polarization meta-analysis (1986); deliberating-enclaves work <br><sub>Isenberg; Sunstein</sub> | The drift: a like-minded group, deliberating only among itself, moving to a more extreme version of its prior view. The Polarisation. |
| ↳ | `the_filter_bubble` | Republic.com (2001); #Republic (2017); "The Law of Group Polarization" (2002) | The consequence: the enclave of the like-minded that hears only itself and drifts to the edge, and the shared public square it fragments. The Echo, the Polarisation. |
| **Surowiecki** | `information_cascade` | The Wisdom of Crowds (2004) <br><sub>Surowiecki (counterpoint)</sub> | The condition and the cure: crowds are wise only when judgements are independent and aggregated, which cascades destroy, so pooling private signals is the remedy. | **Sutherland** | `safety_in_numbers` | "What is the Allee effect?" (Oikos, 1999) <br><sub>Stephens, Sutherland & Freckleton</sub> | The definition: the Allee effect as a positive relationship between per-capita growth rate and density, and the critical density that marks it. The Work. |
| **Swets** | `alarm_fatigue` | Signal Detection Theory and Psychophysics (1966) <br><sub>Green and Swets</sub> | The threshold: detection is a criterion set against a base rate, so cutting misses raises false alarms, the trade the whole trap turns on. The Threshold. |
| **Tainter** | `the_last_layer` | The Collapse of Complex Societies (Cambridge University Press, 1988) <br><sub>Joseph A. Tainter</sub> | The origin and the thesis: complexity is a problem-solving strategy with a declining marginal return, so a society that answers every problem by adding complexity reaches a point where the apparatus costs more than it yields and simplifies by collapse. The play's governing law. |
| **Tajfel** | `not_invented_here` | social identity theory (1979; 1986) <br><sub>Tajfel & Turner</sub> | The substrate: in-group and out-group, the identity boundary that trusts the inside and suspects the outside. The Boundary. |
| **Takeuchi** | `the_bus_factor` | "The Knowledge-Creating Company" (1995) <br><sub>Nonaka & Takeuchi</sub> | Tacit versus explicit knowledge and the cost of converting one to the other: why capturing what an expert knows is slow, effortful, and easily deferred. |
| **Taleb** | `the_brittle_optimum` | The Black Swan (2007); Antifragile (2012) | The fragility: stripping redundancy and volatility hides fragility that a rare shock reveals; the turkey fed until the day it is not. The Slack, the Insurance. |
| ↳ | `the_survivors` | Fooled by Randomness (2001); The Black Swan (2007), the problem of silent evidence | The graveyard: the losers of a random process leave no record, so the visible winners make skill out of luck. The Sea, and the reading of the absent. |
| **Tardos** | `the_extra_road` | "How Bad Is Selfish Routing?", Journal of the ACM (2002) <br><sub>Roughgarden & Tardos</sub> | The bound: the price of anarchy of selfish routing is bounded, and Braess configurations are where it bites; pricing recovers the optimum. | **Tayler** | `metric_fixation` | "Lost in Translation: The Effects of Incentive Compensation on Strategy Surrogation", The Accounting Review (2012) <br><sub>Choi, Hecht & Tayler</sub> | Surrogation: under incentives, decision-makers come to treat the metric as the construct it proxies, not merely to optimise it. The metric becoming the mission, measured. |
| **Taylor** | `sandbag` | The Principles of Scientific Management (1911), on "systematic soldiering" | Output restriction: workers deliberately hold their pace below capacity so the rate is not cut, and enforce it on each other. The Soldiering, and the shop-floor half of the ratchet. |
| **Teasdale** | `the_open_cage` | "Learned helplessness in humans: Critique and reformulation" (J. Abnormal Psychology, 1978) <br><sub>Abramson, Seligman & Teasdale</sub> | The attribution: passivity credited to internal, stable, global causes, the reading of the giving-up as character. The Verdict. |
| **Teger** | `good_money_after_bad` | "Too Much Invested to Quit" (1980) | The entrapment dynamic: the receding payoff and the rising cost of quitting that keep a party committed past the point of sense. The Receding Finish. |
| ↳ | `the_dollar_auction` | Too Much Invested to Quit (Pergamon Press, 1980) <br><sub>Allan Teger</sub> | The experimental entrapment: real subjects run through the dollar auction, escalating well past the prize, and the two motives that drive them, the economic wish to recoup and the interpersonal wish to win, documented in the laboratory. | **Tesser** | `watermelon_reporting` | "On Reluctance to Communicate Undesirable Information: The MUM Effect", Sociometry (1970); field study, Psychological Reports (1971) <br><sub>Rosen & Tesser</sub> | Origin of the mum effect: senders are reluctant to transmit bad news to a recipient, even when not at fault. | **Tetlock** | `the_paper_trail` | "Accountability: The Neglected Social Context of Judgment and Choice" (1985); the accountability studies | The mechanism: accountability to an audience induces justification-seeking, and under blame — pre-committed, or for a failure already in hand — it hardens into defensive, self-protective decision-making. | **Thaler** | `the_winning_bid` | "Anomalies: The Winner's Curse", Journal of Economic Perspectives (1988) | The synthesis and evidence: the curse as a robust behavioural anomaly across auctions, and why bidders fail to shade enough. The Curse, the Bid. |
| **Thompson** | `everybodys_business` | "Moral Responsibility of Public Officials: The Problem of Many Hands", American Political Science Review (1980) | The problem of many hands: when many contribute to an outcome, no single person is answerable for it, so the failure has no author. The Many Hands and the Orphan. |
| **Tibshirani** | `overfitting` | The Elements of Statistical Learning (2001), training vs test error <br><sub>Hastie, Tibshirani and Friedman</sub> | The fitting curve: training error falling monotonically while test error turns back up. The Fit, the Holdout. |
| **Tiebout** | `the_lowest_bar` | "A Pure Theory of Local Expenditures" (Journal of Political Economy, 1956) | The engine: mobility and jurisdictional competition, the mobile party sorting to the jurisdiction whose terms suit it. The Mobility. |
| **Tikhonov** | `overfitting` | Tikhonov regularisation (1943); Akaike Information Criterion (1974) <br><sub>Tikhonov; Akaike</sub> | The penalty on complexity: a cost added for capacity to pull the fit back toward the signal. The Regularising, the Retuning. |
| **Tirole** | `regulatory_capture` | "The Politics of Government Decision-Making: A Theory of Regulatory Capture", QJE (1991) <br><sub>Laffont & Tirole</sub> | The formal model: information asymmetry and the revolving door as the levers by which the regulated capture the regulator. | **Trevor** | `the_dead_sea_effect` | "Voluntary Turnover and Job Performance", Journal of Applied Psychology (1997) <br><sub>Trevor, Gerhart & Boudreau</sub> | The empirical link: high performers leave more readily when pay and promotion do not match their market value, so conditions sort the able out. | **Tsebelis** | `vetocracy` | Veto Players: How Political Institutions Work (2002) | The formal theory: the more veto players, and the more distant, the greater the policy stability and gridlock. The Thicket. |

The play's governing law. |
| ↳ | `rent_seeking` | Toward a Theory of the Rent-Seeking Society (1980); with Bhagwati's "directly unproductive" activities (1982) <br><sub>Buchanan, Tollison & Tullock</sub> | The systematisation: rent-seeking versus profit-seeking, and the constitutional aim of shrinking the transfers that call it forth. The load-bearing equal acceptance. |
| ↳ | `pournelles_iron_law` | Economy and Society (1922); the permanent bureaucratic apparatus | The apparatus: the permanent officialdom whose careers bind them to the organisation's machinery rather than its ends. The Custodian, the Maintenance. |

| ↳ | `the_winning_bid` | "A Theory of Auctions and Competitive Bidding", Econometrica (1982) <br><sub>Milgrom & Weber</sub> | The theory: how information and affiliation drive equilibrium bids, and why the curse deepens as the field grows. The Spread, the Selection. |

| **Weisbrod** | `the_tyranny_of_small_decisions` | "Collective-Consumption Services of Individual-Consumption Goods" (QJE, 1964), option value | The value lost: the worth of an alternative's availability, held even by those who do not use it. The Option Value. |

| ↳ | `information_cascade` | "Sequential Sales, Learning, and Cascades", Journal of Finance (1992) | The market case: early buyers set a cascade later ones follow, so demand herds on the first few. The First, the Tipping Point. |

The Exception. |
| **Wiggins** | `nothing_to_lose` | "Liability and Large-Scale, Long-Term Hazards" (Journal of Political Economy, 1990) <br><sub>Ringleb & Wiggins</sub> | Hazardous activity spun into thin subsidiaries: dangerous work contracted out to small, under-capitalised firms to cap exposure. The Thinness. |
| **Wilde** | `the_spent_margin` | "The Theory of Risk Homeostasis: Implications for Safety and Health" (Risk Analysis, 1982); Target Risk (1994) <br><sub>Gerald J. S. Wilde</sub> | The mechanism: a target level of accepted risk that behaviour defends, so a change in one channel is compensated until perceived risk returns to target. The Balance. |
| **Williams** | `everybodys_business` | "Many Hands Make Light the Work: The Causes and Consequences of Social Loafing", JPSP (1979); the Ringelmann effect <br><sub>Latané, Williams & Harkins</sub> | Effort and felt responsibility per person decline as the group grows, the same division of a fixed duty across more heads. The Thinning. |
| ↳ | `social_loafing` | "Many Hands Make Light the Work: The Causes and Consequences of Social Loafing", JPSP (1979) <br><sub>Latané, Williams & Harkins</sub> | The naming: effort per person falls when contributions are pooled and unidentifiable; reduced identifiability and dispensability as causes. The Pool, the Dispensable Hand. |
| ↳ | `social_loafing` | "Social Loafing: A Meta-Analytic Review and Theoretical Integration", JPSP (1993) <br><sub>Karau & Williams</sub> | The collective effort model: loafing shrinks when contributions are identifiable, the task is meaningful, and each input is felt to matter. The remedy, the Name Tag. |
| **Wilson** | `the_market_for_lemons` | "The Nature of Equilibrium in Markets with Adverse Selection", Bell Journal of Economics (1980) | The unravelling dynamic formalised: how a market with hidden quality can spiral down through its own price. The Unraveling. |
| ↳ | `the_winning_bid` | "Competitive Bidding with Disparate Information" (1969); common-value auction theory | The model: with a common value and private noisy signals, equilibrium bidding requires bidding well below one's estimate. The Estimate, the Shade. |
| **Windle** | `the_balloon_effect` | "Popping the Balloon Effect: Assessing Drug Law Enforcement in Terms of Displacement, Diffusion, and the Containment Hypothesis" (2012) <br><sub>Windle and Farrell</sub> | The honest limit: displacement is often partial, diffusion of benefits is possible, and containment is contested. The credited caveat on the claim. |
| **Winkler** | `the_best_on_paper` | "The Optimizer's Curse: Skepticism and Postdecision Surprise in Decision Analysis" (Management Science, 2006) <br><sub>Smith & Winkler</sub> | The origin and the name: choosing the option with the highest of noisy but unbiased estimates biases the chosen estimate high, so the realised value on average disappoints, and the Bayesian remedy is to shrink estimates toward the prior. The play's governing law. The Letdown. |
| **Winship** | `the_false_trade_off` | "Endogenous Selection Bias: The Problem of Conditioning on a Collider Variable" (Annual Review of Sociology, 2014) <br><sub>Elwert & Winship</sub> | The general statement: across the social sciences, conditioning on a collider, including selecting a sample on an outcome the variables jointly cause, manufactures association where none exists, a hazard as general as confounding and less recognised. The Reading. |
| **Winter** | `the_lowest_bar` | "State Law, Shareholder Protection, and the Theory of the Corporation" (Journal of Legal Studies, 1977) | The counter: where the mobile party bears the cost of a lax standard, competition can raise standards, a race to the top. The Externality, the honest hinge. |
| **Woods** | `the_irony_of_automation` | "Team Play with a Powerful and Independent Agent" (Human Factors, 1997); automation surprises, out-of-the-loop <br><sub>Sarter and Woods</sub> | The handover: the operator surprised by an automation they no longer track, taking over in an abnormal state. The Handover, the Cold Seat. |
| **Wooldridge** | `the_frozen_middle` | "The Strategic Middle Manager" (1996); "Middle Management's Strategic Influence and Organizational Performance", JMS (1997) <br><sub>Floyd & Wooldridge</sub> | The middle's four roles, implementing and facilitating downward, synthesising and championing upward: the same seats carry the change down and the reports up. The two-way structure. |
| **Yandle** | `bootleggers_and_baptists` | "Bootleggers and Baptists: The Education of a Regulatory Economist" (Regulation, 1983) <br><sub>Bruce Yandle</sub> | The origin and the name: the durable regulation backed by a moral constituency for its principle and a private one for its profit, the Sabbath ban that honours the moralist and shelters the bootlegger, wanted by both for reasons that never touch. The play's governing law. |
| ↳ | `bootleggers_and_baptists` | Bootleggers and Baptists: How Economic Forces and Moral Persuasion Interact to Shape Regulatory Politics (Bruce Yandle & Adam C. Smith, Cato Institute, 2014) <br><sub>Yandle & Smith</sub> | The taxonomy: the book-length treatment, sorting the coalitions by whether the two sides are covert or coordinated and showing how moral persuasion and economic interest interact across cases from Prohibition to modern regulation. The Cover. |
| **Yang** | `the_tyranny_of_the_urgent` | "The Mere Urgency Effect" (Journal of Consumer Research, 2018) <br><sub>Zhu, Yang, and Hsee</sub> | The spine: people choose urgent-but-less-rewarding tasks over important ones merely for the sense of expiration, urgency capturing effort against payoff. The governing law. |
| **Yankelovich** | `the_streetlight_effect` | the McNamara fallacy, as named in Corporate Priorities (1972) | The measurable over the important: measuring what is easily measured and dismissing the rest as unreal. The Light, the False Find. |
| **Yoon** | `the_anticommons` | "Symmetric Tragedies: Commons and Anticommons", Journal of Law and Economics (2000) <br><sub>Buchanan & Yoon</sub> | The symmetry proved: the commons underprices access and is overused; the anticommons overprices it and is underused. The two are one structure inverted. The play's mirror to The Commons. |
| **Yule** | `the_bigger_picture` | "Notes on the Theory of Association of Attributes in Statistics" (1903); the Yule-Simpson effect <br><sub>G. Udny Yule</sub> | The precedent: the earliest statement of the association reversal on aggregation. The Reversal. |
| **Zhong** | `moral_licensing` | "Do Green Products Make Us Better People?" (Psychological Science, 2010) <br><sub>Mazar & Zhong</sub> | The cross-domain lapse: buying green then cheating and stealing more, a good deed spent elsewhere. The Lapse. |
| **Zhu** | `the_tyranny_of_the_urgent` | "The Mere Urgency Effect" (Journal of Consumer Research, 2018) <br><sub>Zhu, Yang, and Hsee</sub> | The spine: people choose urgent-but-less-rewarding tasks over important ones merely for the sense of expiration, urgency capturing effort against payoff. The governing law. |
| **Zucker** | `the_done_thing` | "Institutional Sources of Change in the Formal Structure of Organizations" (ASQ, 1983) <br><sub>Tolbert & Zucker</sub> | Legitimacy over efficiency: later adopters take the form for legitimacy after any efficiency case has passed. The Fit. |
| **Zuckerman** | `the_head_start` | Harriet Zuckerman, "Scientific Elite" (1977); the "forty-first chair" and eponymy <br><sub>Merton & Zuckerman / Zuckerman</sub> | The credit: how recognition concentrates on a visible elite while comparable others go unnamed. The Byline, the Chronicler. |
